methodologies.name: Crawl-Walk-Run Automation Gating
methodologies.summary: Roll an agent out in three explicit autonomy tiers with explicit gates between them. Crawl emits only suggestions, a human acts. Walk acts on internal staff who can correct it. Run acts directly on external customers. Tier is per-action-type, not per-agent — the same agent can be in Run for low-risk read actions and Crawl for refunds. Promotion requires clearing a published metric bar; regression demotes automatically.

diagram.mermaid:
stateDiagram-v2
  [*] --> Crawl: every action type starts here
  Crawl --> Walk: clears acceptance-rate bar for hold duration
  Walk --> Run: clears customer-outcome bar for hold duration
  Walk --> Crawl: regression below Crawl-Walk bar (with hysteresis)
  Run --> Walk: regression below Walk-Run customer-outcome bar
  Run --> Crawl: severe regression / incident-driven demote
  note right of Crawl
    Agent emits suggestions only
    Human accepts / rejects / edits
  end note
  note right of Walk
    Agent acts on internal staff
    Internal staff can correct
  end note
  note right of Run
    Agent acts on external customers
    Customer-outcome metric monitored
  end note

methodologies.example_scenario:
A mid-size retailer's customer-support team is deploying an AI agent that can answer questions, look up orders, and issue refunds. They enumerate four action types up front: 'reply to FAQ-style ticket', 'lookup order status' (read-only), 'issue refund up to $50', 'issue refund above $50'. They publish per-tier bars: Crawl-Walk requires 90% human acceptance over 200 actions in a two-week window; Walk-Run requires a customer-outcome metric (re-open rate within 7 days < 4%) over 500 actions in three weeks.

On day one every action type sits at Crawl. The agent drafts; humans accept, edit, or reject. After two weeks, FAQ replies hit 94% acceptance and lookup hits 99% — both promote to Walk. Lookup also clears the customer-outcome bar within a further three weeks (re-open rate flat) and promotes to Run. FAQ replies sit at Walk longer because the re-open rate creeps up to 5% on edge cases involving order modifications; the action type stays at Walk pending fixes.

The refund actions move separately. Refund-up-to-$50 reaches Walk after a month. Refund-above-$50 stays at Crawl indefinitely by team policy, regardless of acceptance, because the blast radius justifies a permanent human-in-the-loop. Six weeks in, a prompt change inadvertently regressed FAQ acceptance to 81%; the system auto-demoted FAQ back to Crawl, the team rolled back the prompt, and a week later FAQ returned to Walk. Nobody framed the demotion as a failure — it was the gate doing exactly what it was designed to do.
methodologies.intent: Decouple capability from autonomy so a system that can plausibly act gets to act only after the data justifies it, per action type.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Any agent that could plausibly act autonomously on customer-facing actions — reply to tickets, refund orders, send outbound messages, modify production resources. The blast radius of a single bad action is non-trivial and there is no reliable undo. Don't apply to read-only or sandboxed agents where the blast radius is zero.

inputs.name: Catalog of action types
inputs.description: An enumerated list of distinct action types the agent can emit, each with its own blast radius.

inputs.name: Promotion bar per tier
inputs.description: Published thresholds (acceptance rate, internal completion, customer-outcome) that an action type must clear to advance to the next tier.

inputs.name: Per-action-type metric pipeline
inputs.description: Telemetry that records, for every action, which tier it ran at and what happened next.

outputs.name: Per-action-type tier assignment
outputs.description: Live mapping from action type to current tier (Crawl / Walk / Run).

outputs.name: Promotion log
outputs.description: An auditable record of which action types advanced/regressed, when, and on what evidence.

steps.name: Enumerate action types
steps.description: List the discrete action types the agent can take. 'Reply to ticket', 'issue refund up to $50', 'issue refund above $50', 'escalate to human' are four distinct types, not one.

steps.name: Publish the metric bar per tier
steps.description: For each tier, write down the metric (acceptance rate, completion rate, customer outcome) and the threshold that must be cleared to advance. Pin the duration each tier must hold before promotion is considered.

steps.name: Start every action type at Crawl
steps.description: On day one, no action type is in Walk or Run regardless of how the system was developed. Crawl emits suggestions; a human accepts/rejects/edits.

steps.name: Promote one action type at a time
steps.description: When an action type clears the Crawl→Walk bar, promote that one type. The agent now acts on internal staff for that action; everything else stays at Crawl.

steps.name: Watch for regression and auto-demote
steps.description: If a tier's metric falls below its bar (with a hysteresis margin to avoid flapping), demote that action type back. The demotion is not a failure event — it's the system working.

steps.name: Advance to Run with the customer-outcome metric
steps.description: Walk→Run requires not just internal acceptance but a customer-facing outcome metric (resolved ticket without re-open, refund not reversed, message not flagged). Internal acceptance ≠ external success.

principles: Autonomy is a property of action types, not of the agent.
Every tier publishes both a metric bar and a duration — duration prevents one good week from advancing a risky action.
Promotion is one-action-at-a-time; regression is automatic.
Internal acceptance is not customer success — the Walk→Run gate uses a different metric than Crawl→Walk.

known_failure_modes.note: Shipping the agent fully autonomous to external customers from day one because internal demos looked good — behaviour that cannot be retracted.
Hiding the agent behind a human reviewer forever and never establishing the promotion bar — the agent never gets to add value.

sources.title: AI Engineering
sources.chapter: Ch 10 'AI Engineering Architecture and User Feedback'
sources.quote: Architecture Steps: Enhance Context, Put in Guardrails, Add Model Router and Gateway, Reduce Latency with Caches, Add Agent Patterns

sources.title: Building A Generative AI Platform (Chip Huyen, author's blog precursor to AI Engineering Ch 10)
sources.quote: The initial expansion of a platform usually involves adding mechanisms to allow the system to augment each query with the necessary information ... Guardrails help reduce AI risks and protect not just your users but also you, the developers ... An application can use different models to respond to different types of queries

sources.title: AI Engineering Architecture and User Feedback (Alex Strick van Linschoten)
sources.quote: User feedback systems inherently contain various biases ... negative experience bias and self-selection bias

methodologies.name: Shadow Canary Bandit Rollout
methodologies.summary: Promote an agent change through escalating exposure stages: shadow (runs alongside production with no user impact and is compared offline), canary (a small slice of real traffic at full risk), then Bayesian bandits or A/B that allocate traffic by observed reward. Each stage is a gate — regression at any stage halts the rollout and triggers regression-trace collection. Distinct from a tier-based autonomy ladder because the unit of promotion here is an individual change (prompt, model, retrieval tweak), not an action type.

diagram.mermaid:
stateDiagram-v2
  [*] --> Shadow: candidate change submitted
  Shadow --> Canary: shadow diff acceptable, no latency/error regression
  Shadow --> Halted: gross output drift or crash
  Canary --> Bandit: clears canary exit criteria on 1-5% slice
  Canary --> Halted: any exit criterion violated
  Bandit --> Promoted: posterior reward sustained above incumbent
  Bandit --> Halted: posterior reward falls below incumbent with confidence
  Halted --> Shadow: fixed build resubmitted
  Halted --> [*]: change abandoned
  Promoted --> [*]
  note right of Halted
    Regression traces captured
    Folded into eval set
  end note

methodologies.example_scenario:
A team running a high-volume customer-support chatbot has a new prompt that they believe handles refund-policy questions better. They submit it as a candidate change. Shadow stage: production traffic is mirrored to the new prompt for 48 hours; outputs are diffed offline against the incumbent. Latency comes in 6ms lower, no crashes, output drift modest and concentrated exactly on refund-policy queries (the targeted improvement). Shadow passes.

Canary stage: 2% of live traffic routes to the new prompt with the frozen exit criteria — minimum 4,000 conversations, no >0.5% absolute error-rate regression, no >5% latency p95 regression, guardrail-trip count not increasing. After 36 hours the criteria are met on every axis, including a 3-point lift on the refund-question subset. Canary passes. Allocation is handed to a Bayesian bandit with the new prompt and the incumbent as the two arms; reward is binary thumbs-up from the user. Within five days the bandit has shifted to 78% allocation to the new prompt because its posterior reward is sustained 4 points above the incumbent.

A week later, an unrelated retrieval change is canary-tested and fails — the canary error rate spiked because the retriever returned empty results on a class of queries the shadow comparison hadn't surfaced. The build is halted, the failing traces are bundled and added to the regression eval set, and the change goes back for rework. The team didn't roll back quietly — the trace bundle is exactly what's needed to write the next round of tests, so the failure produces a permanent improvement to the harness rather than just an embarrassed Slack thread.
methodologies.intent: Promote agent changes through staged exposure (shadow then canary then bandit/A-B) so production exposure scales with observed reward, with regression auto-halting promotion.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Any production agent or LLM application where changes ship continuously and bad changes can degrade user experience or cost. Especially when traffic is high enough to learn from (canary needs enough volume to detect regressions). Don't apply to low-volume systems where Bayesian bandits cannot accumulate enough signal — fall back to plain shadow plus manual cutover.

inputs.name: Production traffic stream
inputs.description: Live requests that can be mirrored to a shadow build and split for canary.

inputs.name: Reward signal
inputs.description: A measurable outcome per request — user thumbs-up, task completion, downstream metric — that the bandit can allocate against.

inputs.name: Regression detector
inputs.description: Automated checks that flag when a new build's reward or error rate falls below the incumbent's.

outputs.name: Promotion log
outputs.description: Auditable record of which change passed which stage on what evidence.

outputs.name: Regression trace bundle
outputs.description: Captured traces from any failed stage, ready for offline debugging.

outputs.name: Traffic allocation policy
outputs.description: Current bandit posterior or A/B split deciding how much traffic each variant gets.

steps.name: Shadow the new build
steps.description: Mirror production traffic to the new build. Compare outputs and metrics offline. No user impact. Catches obvious crashes, latency regressions, and gross output drift before any user sees the change.

steps.name: Define the canary slice and exit criteria
steps.description: Pick a small live slice (1–5%) and write down what 'pass' looks like: minimum sample size, allowed regression on each metric, time window. The criteria are frozen before the canary opens.

steps.name: Open the canary
steps.description: Route the slice to the new build at full risk. Watch error rate, latency, reward signal, and any guardrail-trip count.

steps.name: Collect regression traces on any failure
steps.description: If the canary fails any exit criterion, halt promotion and capture the failing traces in a regression bundle. The bundle becomes a new eval-set entry for the next iteration.

steps.name: Promote to bandit or A/B
steps.description: When the canary clears its bar, hand allocation to a Bayesian bandit (or fixed A/B if simpler). The bandit grows the new build's traffic share in proportion to its observed reward advantage.

steps.name: Watch posteriors and auto-rollback on regression
steps.description: Monitor the bandit posterior. If the new build's posterior reward falls below the incumbent with confidence, the bandit naturally allocates traffic away; couple this with an explicit rollback threshold to fully demote the build.

principles: Exposure escalates only on evidence — every stage gates the next.
Regressions are first-class outputs — captured as traces and folded into the eval set, not just rolled back.
Bandits replace fixed A/B once traffic is high enough; below that volume, A/B or canary-with-manual-cutover is the honest choice.
Every stage has a published exit criterion frozen before the stage opens.

known_failure_modes.note: Skipping shadow and canary because the new build 'looked great in dev' — the cliff is exactly what shadow is for.
Stage gates set so high that no change ever advances past canary, freezing the system in indefinite trial mode.
Failing canaries quietly rolled back without capturing the regression trace — the same failure ships again next quarter.

sources.title: Building Applications with AI Agents
sources.quote: Monitoring patterns including 'Shadow Mode' and 'Canary Deployments' ... 'Regression Trace Collection' ... Experimentation approaches: 'Shadow Deployments,' 'A/B Testing,' and 'Bayesian Bandits'

sources.title: LLM Canary Prompting in Production: Shadow Tests, Drift Alarms, and Safe Rollouts
sources.quote: Treat prompt changes like deployments, and wrap them in the same safety rails you'd give any other production rollout.

methodologies.name: Five-Level Agent Progression
methodologies.summary: Six-rung maturity ladder for AI agent capability — Level 0 Manual, Level 1 Rule-Based, Level 2 Intelligent Process Automation, Level 3 Agentic Workflows, Level 4 Semi-Autonomous, Level 5 Fully Autonomous. Teams pick a target level by task risk and business value, then gate advancement on prior-level guardrails and evaluation passing. Distinct from a per-action-type promotion gate: this is a capability ladder for the agent as a whole, used to align stakeholders on what 'done' means before construction starts.

diagram.mermaid:
stateDiagram-v2
  [*] --> L0: Manual baseline
  L0 --> L1: rules + automation in place
  L1 --> L2: ML triggers added (IPA)
  L2 --> L3: agentic workflow with planning
  L3 --> L4: semi-autonomous with HITL gates
  L4 --> L5: fully autonomous, evals + guardrails proven over months
  L5 --> L4: regression demotes one rung pending fix
  L4 --> L3: regression demotes one rung pending fix
  L3 --> L2: regression demotes one rung pending fix
  note right of L3
    Each promotion requires:
    - prior-level guardrails in place
    - eval set passing at threshold
    - documented evidence
  end note
  note right of L4
    Skipping rungs (e.g. L1->L4)
    ships demos that break
    on production drift
  end note

methodologies.example_scenario:
An insurance carrier is scoping an agent for claims processing across three task families: first-notice-of-loss intake (high volume, low risk per call), document-completeness checks (medium volume, medium risk — wrong call delays payouts), and settlement-amount calculation (low volume, high risk — wrong call costs real money or under-pays customers). The CTO wants L5 'fully autonomous claims agent'. The chief actuary wants L1 'rule-based with human review on everything'. The framework is used to align them.

They map each task family to a candidate level: FNOL intake at L3 (agentic workflow, since multimodal documents and unstructured speech require reasoning), document-completeness at L2 (IPA — ML triggers on existing OCR pipeline), settlement calculation at L1 (rule-based — actuarial tables don't need an agent, and regulators won't accept opaque reasoning). The CTO and actuary both sign off; the target is explicitly mixed across tasks rather than one number for the whole agent.

For FNOL intake — the most ambitious — they list the L2 preconditions: OCR pipeline at 98% field-extraction accuracy on the existing form set, exception-routing rules in place, observability into every call. The L3 advancement criteria are: planner-eval passing at the threshold the agent-platform team published, and 60 days of L2 operating with the published exception rate. Construction proceeds one level at a time. Six months in, the team reviews and confirms FNOL has reached L3 with the named evidence; the actuary asks whether settlement could move to L2 given new explainability tools, and they re-open that question. The ladder is a planning instrument, revisited as portfolio risk shifts, not a static org chart.
methodologies.intent: Place an agent on a six-rung capability ladder so target autonomy and the guardrails required to reach it are explicit before construction.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Early-stage scoping of an agent project, when stakeholders disagree on how autonomous the system should be, or when planning a multi-quarter capability roadmap. Also useful as a retrospective lens — 'we said L4, we shipped L2.5'. Don't apply once construction is underway and the action-type gate (crawl-walk-run) is the right tool; the level ladder is a planning instrument, not a runtime control.

inputs.name: Task portfolio
inputs.description: The set of tasks the agent will handle, with risk and value annotations per task.

inputs.name: Stakeholder constraints
inputs.description: Regulatory, organisational, and risk-tolerance constraints on autonomy.

inputs.name: Prior-level evidence
inputs.description: Evals and incident history from the level the system currently operates at.

outputs.name: Target level declaration
outputs.description: Per-task or per-system target on the L0–L5 ladder, with rationale.

outputs.name: Advancement criteria
outputs.description: Named guardrails, evals, and operational signals required to advance one level.

outputs.name: Capability roadmap
outputs.description: Sequenced plan to move from current level to target level.

steps.name: Map tasks to candidate levels
steps.description: For each task in the portfolio, identify the natural level: pure manual (L0), rule-based automation (L1), IPA with ML triggers (L2), agentic workflow with planning (L3), semi-autonomous with HITL gates (L4), fully autonomous (L5). Task risk and value drive the choice, not technical excitement.

steps.name: Declare a target level per task or system
steps.description: Write down the target. Stakeholders sign off explicitly. Mixed targets across tasks are normal — a customer-support agent may be L4 for FAQ and L2 for refunds.

steps.name: Define prior-level guardrails as preconditions
steps.description: For each level, list the guardrails and evals that must already pass at the lower level before advancement is on the table. L4 → L5 typically requires that L4 has logged months of HITL acceptance at the published threshold.

steps.name: Build to one level above current
steps.description: Advance one rung at a time. Skipping levels (L1 → L4 directly) tends to ship demos that fail under production drift.

steps.name: Gate advancement on evidence
steps.description: Promote only when the named guardrails are in place and the eval set passes at the threshold. Document the evidence. Regression at a higher level demotes the system one rung pending fix.

steps.name: Revisit target level on portfolio shifts
steps.description: When task risk or business value changes (new regulation, new product line), re-evaluate the target level. The ladder is a planning instrument, not a permanent label.

principles: Autonomy level is a planning choice driven by task risk and value, not technical capability alone.
Advance one level at a time — skipping rungs ships demos that production breaks.
Higher levels require lower-level guardrails to already be in place.
The ladder is a stakeholder-alignment artefact, not a runtime control — pair it with action-type gating at runtime.

known_failure_modes.note: Declaring L5 as the target on day one because the demo looked autonomous — guardrails for L1–L4 never get built.
Skipping rungs collapses on first production drift; the eval at the destination level never existed.
Never advancing past L3 because no team owns building the next-level guardrails.

sources.title: Agentic Artificial Intelligence: Harnessing AI Agents to Reinvent Business, Work, and Life
sources.chapter: Ch 3 'The Five Levels of AI Agents: From Automation to Autonomy' (pp. 61–81)
sources.quote: The Five Levels Of AI Agents: From Automation To Autonomy (pages 61-81)

sources.title: Truly Useful AI: Reviewing Pascal Bornet's Five-Level Guide to Getting Stuff Done with Agents
sources.quote: Level 0 (Manual) ... Level 1 (Rule-Based): Basic automation following fixed rules ... Level 2 (Intelligent Process Automation) ... Level 3 (Agentic Workflows) ... Level 4 (Semi-Autonomous) ... Level 5 (Fully Autonomous)
